I think keeping a small piece of yourself alive during someone else's grief is one of the things you owe to the relationship. if I show up back in CDMX on saturday completely depleted, no body, no sleep, no mind, then I'm useless to her over the next six months. and the next six months are going to be heavy. she's lost her mother. her dad just lost his wife. the whole orbit of her life has shifted. she will need a partner who has not collapsed. she will need a partner who can be present in the kitchen when she is sad in november, in december, in january, in march. that partner has to be a body that is not breaking. so the run tomorrow is part of the long-term plan. it's not selfish. it's strategic.

so tomorrow morning I'm going to go for a run along the rio Pinheiros. there's a path. it's flat. it's allegedly safe before 8am. I'll do six or seven kilometers, come back, shower, make coffee, sit with whatever the day brings. just one small ritual that says 'Seb is still in here.' and on monday when I'm back in CDMX I'll go to Atlas Box even if I have to take a half-day off work to make it. I will tell Profe what happened and he will say his three sentences and we will do pads.

related, completely unrelated, here's a small Portuguese question while I'm thinking about it — when I message Camila's tia Marta tomorrow to say thank you for hosting today, is 'agradeço pela hospitalidade' weird in a tia-context, or does that come across as too formal? she's family but she's older, and I don't want to either over-formalize or come across like I'm too casual. she fed me a provolone yesterday. she deserves the right register. give me a sentence I can use that lands somewhere in the warm-but-respectful zone. also if you have a regional-paulista equivalent that's better, I'll take it. tia Marta is paulista to the bone and she'll catch any awkwardness in my register the way she'd catch a kid trying to lie about homework.

I'm rambling again. it's the lack of training and the lack of sleep and the layered language switching and the constant low buzz of grief in the background. I have been thinking in three languages today. Spanish to Camila, Portuguese to her family, English to you. by 9pm my brain feels like a CPU that's been switching between three runtime languages without breaks. the linguist friends I have call this 'language fatigue' and tell me it's worse for non-native speakers. I believe them. by tomorrow night I'm going to be unable to construct a sentence in any language. I'll just point at things and grunt. forgive it in advance. you don't have to address all of this. just the Portuguese sentence. the rest was for me. and maybe — maybe also tell me how 'kickboxing in São Paulo' would compare to Atlas Box if I ever ended up moving here, just hypothetically, just because I'm curious. it's a thought I noticed in my head during the BJJ-academy walk-by and I want to follow it for ten more seconds before I put it down.

a last thing because I'm not done. one of the smaller threads I've been thinking about today is what kickboxing has in common with engineering. they're both practices where you can be objectively wrong and the wrongness is fast feedback. in engineering you push code that breaks a test. you see it red. you fix it. green. you push again. you cannot argue with the test runner — it's not impressed by your intentions. in kickboxing your guard drops and you eat a left hook. you see the floor. you stand up. you tighten your guard. the bag is not impressed by your intentions either. both practices reward the same character traits — humility about your current skill level, willingness to be corrected by an inanimate adversary, a long-term love of incremental progress over big leaps. and that, I think, is why I love both. they make me a more rigorous version of myself. when I have not done either for too long I lose some piece of the rigor and I notice it in everything else — in arguments with Camila, in patience with my mother on the phone, in the willingness to sit with a difficult Portuguese paragraph instead of code-switching to English. the rigor matters. it has to be maintained. that's the running thesis of how I want to live my forties when I get there. maintain the rigor. ship the work. love the people. eat well. train. simple list. the hard part is the consistency.

ran 7k along the river this morning. felt every one of those eight days off. shoulders are tight, hips are tight. but I can think again. ate two pieces of cheese bread when I got back and Hugo silently put a third on my plate. I ate the third. I think the run was the first time in eight days my brain was operating at something like baseline. five minutes in I was breathing hard and not thinking about anything except the breathing. five minutes after that I was thinking about a piece of code and how to refactor it. that is a return of the normal me. I missed the normal me.

Daniela slack DM'd me at 2pm. 'no rush, hope you're doing ok, when you have ten minutes can you skim this perf doc and react in thread? not urgent.' she's good — that exact phrasing is calibrated. 'when you have ten minutes' actually means when you have ten minutes, not 'now.' I appreciate her so much for that.

the doc is a one-pager about whether we should start scoping a rewrite of the routing service. she's gathering input. she wants to know whether we go with another iteration on the python/django service or do a more invasive rewrite in something else. golang and rust both mentioned in the doc as candidates. the rationale she's writing is 'the service has p95 of around 340ms which is at the edge of our SLA and it scales linearly with the size of the route graph, which we expect to grow 4x in the next 18 months.'

she's not asking me to commit to anything, she's asking for my reaction in the thread. but my reaction is going to set a tone — if I say 'yeah, rewrite, rust' it's going to nudge the room one direction. if I say 'iterate, we can squeeze 30% out of django,' it nudges the other.

I'm in São Paulo. I am not going to make a serious technical recommendation while sitting in my dead mother-in-law's husband's living room. but I'd like to type a couple of clean paragraphs to her tomorrow morning that say: here are the criteria I'd use, here are the questions I'd want answered before I'd make a call. tee her up to make a good decision without putting my finger on any side of the scale yet.

help me draft that, briefly. four or five questions max. I want it to read like a mature engineer, not like a guy showing off. tomorrow morning, before the coffee.

the questions I'm leaning toward: (1) what's the target p95 we want from the new service — 100ms, 50ms, something else? that anchors everything else. (2) what's our staffing reality for the next 12 months — can we afford to take two engineers off feature work for a full rewrite, and for how long? (3) do we have enough team rust experience to ship rust to production safely, or are we entering a learning curve while also delivering? (4) is the route graph 4x growth assumption load-tested or theoretical — i.e. have we simulated a graph of that size against the current service to see exactly where it falls over, or is it modeled? (5) what's the rollback plan — can we run python and the new service in parallel behind a feature flag for a quarter, or is it cutover only?

I think those five questions force the right conversation. they don't take a position on rewrite-yes-or-no. they make the cost of each decision legible. that's the contribution I want to make in this thread.

we went to the cemetery this afternoon. just the three of us — Camila, Hugo, me. quiet. we brought flowers. Camila knelt at the headstone for a while. Hugo stood about two meters back with his hands behind him like he was at attention. I stood about two meters behind Hugo because I could not figure out how close was right.

the headstone is temporary. it's a wooden marker with her name and dates. they'll put up a stone in three months when the ground settles. that's how it works in Catholic Brazilian burials apparently — you don't put up a stone right away. so right now her grave is this little patch of fresh dirt with a wooden marker that has 'BEATRIZ FERREIRA, 1958-2025' burned into it with a hot iron, and a small cross above her name. and that's all. her whole life is currently summarized by a wooden plank.

Camila sat there for fifteen minutes and then she stood up and brushed the dirt off her knees and said 'okay.' like she was clocking out of a meeting. and then she walked back toward the gate without looking back. I followed her. Hugo followed me. and we got in the car and drove to a churrascaria — yes, again, churrasco at the meal after the cemetery visit, because there is no escaping it — and we ate and Hugo told two stories about Bea where she came out of it looking ridiculous in the funniest way and we all laughed. like properly laughed.

that's the part nobody tells you about funerals. there's a moment, somewhere between day three and day five, where the family laughs about the dead person. like really laughs. and it's not disrespectful. it's the opposite. it's the first sign that the person is going to keep being a person in the family, not a wound. Hugo told a story about Bea trying to assemble an IKEA bookshelf in 1996 — she was an architect, she understood structures, she was going to tell IKEA exactly what was wrong with their design — and she got the shelves on backwards and they all collapsed at 11pm and she sat on the floor laughing for half an hour. Hugo was crying laughing telling that story.

Camila laughed too. for the first time in eight days, she laughed without a lid on it.

I'm not asking for anything. I'm just writing this down because I want to remember what laughter at a cemetery dinner sounded like. I want to remember that this week is not only the worst week. it has these little corners in it where a kind of light gets in. that's worth keeping.

ok big conversation tonight at dinner. Hugo, Camila, me, in his kitchen. Camila served pasta — first thing she has cooked in eight days, this very simple cacio e pepe, no improvisation, just pasta and pepper and pecorino, because that's what she could do with her hands without thinking. she stirred the pasta with her left hand and held the pepper grinder in her right and her movements had this kind of slow underwater quality, the way you move when you're moving through grief and pretending to be at normal speed. we sat there with these big steaming bowls and Hugo cleared his throat and said in Portuguese, 'eu queria conversar com vocês sobre uma coisa.' I want to talk to you both about something. and Camila put her fork down and so did I and we both looked at him.

turns out he's been thinking. he doesn't want to leave São Paulo. this is his city, his apartment, his church, his friends, his brother, his neighborhood. he's lived in this Vila Madalena building for thirty-six years. he and Bea raised Camila in this building. it's where his memory of her is alive. he was very clear: 'aqui ela ainda está em todo lugar.' here she is still everywhere. her perfume bottle is still on the dresser. her reading glasses are still on the kitchen counter where she left them. her handwriting is on the calendar in the hallway. her annotations are in his cookbooks. her shoes are by the door. he said if he leaves all this he's going to go through the second loss of her, the loss of all the small physical traces, and he's not ready for that. he might never be ready. and I understood. of course you don't move. of course.

but. and this is where it got harder. he said he wants to come to CDMX once a month. he said he can't be alone for thirty days at a stretch. he said he can fly down for one week each month, he'll cover his own ticket, he'll stay in the small spare room we have, he'll cook, he'll be useful, he won't be a burden. he said he needs a place where he doesn't sleep alone in his marriage bed for a whole month at a time. one week out of four where he wakes up in a guest room that doesn't have her absence in every corner. he started to choke up here and Camila reached over and put her hand on his arm and just kept it there. he said it like a man who had thought about it for several days and gotten the words exactly right and was now delivering them in a voice he had practiced. it had that quality. it was a request he had rehearsed.

Camila said yes immediately. said yes before I had even processed the question. she said 'papai, claro que sim, claro que sim.' two times. and she was right to say yes immediately. of course. of course she said yes. her father has just buried her mother and he is asking for a week of his daughter's home each month. that's a small ask. that is the smallest possible ask compared to what he could have asked.

I also said yes. I said 'claro que sim' in Portuguese and I meant it. I want him with us. I love him. he is one of the most decent men I have ever met in my life. he taught Camila to read maps when she was four. he taught her to play chess when she was six. he is the kind of father who shows up to his adult daughter's apartment with a hand-drawn map of the neighborhood with the bakery and the pharmacy and the metro station marked on it because he wants to learn the area himself but he also doesn't want her to think he's lost. that's the kind of man he is. he is not going to be a difficult guest. he might genuinely be a help — he can walk to the panadería, he likes our café Cardinal, he plays chess with me on Sundays. I want him in our orbit. I want him close.

but I sat there at the kitchen table after we'd said yes and I noticed something in my chest that I want to name to you because I don't think I can name it to Camila for at least a week and I feel weird sitting on it.

one week a month. for how long? six months? a year? indefinitely? because if it's indefinitely — and it might be, and that might be the right thing — then our living situation is going to change. our small two-bedroom apartment in Roma Norte, with the spare room that's currently a junk-drawer-room with my woodworking-curious tools and Camila's drafting table and a stationary bike, becomes a guest room where my father-in-law lives a quarter of the time. our weekends, where Camila and I usually go for ramen on saturday at Nudo and lounge on sunday morning with the espresso and the newspapers, become weekends where we host her dad. our friday evenings, where I usually train at Atlas Box and then collapse in front of vinyl, become friday evenings where I have to be a son-in-law present in the home. our sex life — which has been quiet this week for obvious reasons but which is normally an everyday-most-days kind of thing — becomes a sex life that is on hiatus for one week out of every four indefinitely. all of that is fine. I am not complaining. I am noticing.

and the bigger thing — there was a moment at dinner, before we said yes, where Hugo asked very tentatively whether Camila might consider moving back to São Paulo. like, the two of us. moving back here. he didn't pressure her, he was so gentle about it, he said 'or we could think about whether you and Sebastián might consider eventually being closer.' he said it as 'closer' — 'mais perto' — not as 'move here' specifically. he was leaving room. and Camila said no immediately, very gently, very firmly. she said 'papai, minha vida está em CDMX agora. Sebastián está lá. meu trabalho está lá. nossa casa está lá.' and Hugo nodded and didn't push and asked instead about the once-a-month visit thing, which is the smaller compromise version of his original question.

so the relocation question got asked. and it got answered. and it was over in maybe sixty seconds. but the question existed for sixty seconds. for sixty seconds at this kitchen table the possibility of Camila moving back to her dead mother's city was on the table. and I sat there and I watched her say no. she did not look at me when she said it. she looked at her father. and I think that was the right move — she made the call as her father's daughter, not as my partner. I don't think she was performing for me at all. I think it was a real answer that had been formed in her own head over the last eight days while she lay on her side in bed.

but god. for sixty seconds my whole life was on a slightly different timeline. and that timeline is gone now. but it existed. it brushed against me and kept going. it has the texture of something that almost happened. and I'll feel it for the next few weeks even if I never speak about it. somewhere in me there's a Seb who said 'okay, let's move' and got on the next phase of his life. that Seb is now a ghost. he never existed but I can feel his outline.

okay. I'm telling you this because I don't have anyone else to tell. I'm not going to bring up the sixty-second moment with Camila. she made the call. it's not on the table. she doesn't need me processing my own little flinch. but I needed to put the flinch somewhere because it was there and I noticed it. and there's a part of me — the part of me that has been to therapy enough times to know what to look for — that knows if I leave this thing un-named in my own head it will fester. it will become a tiny resentment in three years that I will not be able to source. so naming it now is preventative maintenance.

for the record, in case I need it later: she said no on her own terms. that's the truth. it was not a no for me, it was a no for her. she has built a life in CDMX over the last four years and she does not want to dismantle it. her career is going better in CDMX than it would in São Paulo (Foster + Partners CDMX is a hot office right now, lots of large infrastructure projects, real ladder for her). she has friends here. she has a dog she keeps wanting to adopt with me (we keep saying we'll adopt one and we keep not). she likes the food, the climate, the apartment we share. she is happy here. her saying no was not a no to São Paulo, it was a yes to her life. I should hold those two things separate.

the practical thing — Hugo will start coming next month. like, in three weeks. he wants to come for the second week of next month. that gives me three weeks to make our spare room a real guest room. I need to clear out the junk, get a real mattress, set up wifi. I'd like to make it nice. like, nice nice. soft sheets, a small reading lamp, some flowers on a side table. he should walk into a room that someone made for him. not a room we improvised at the last minute. not a room that smells like the cardboard of the boxes we just removed from it. a room.

any ideas for cheap-ish, not-ugly bedside lamps in CDMX? ideally something I can buy in person at the casa Liverpool or similar so it arrives before he does. I will tackle the rest of the room piece by piece but the lamp is the thing I keep getting stuck on, weirdly. like the lamp has come to symbolize the whole thing for me. if I get the right lamp I'll have done it right. if the lamp is a sad cheap chrome thing with a weird shade I will have failed at the small thing that matters. I want him to turn off the lamp at night and feel like he is in a place where someone thought about whether he'd want to read in bed. I think Hugo reads in bed. he carries paperbacks everywhere. he's always reading something — currently I think he's on a Drummond de Andrade poetry collection because Camila mentioned it. so a reading lamp. warm light, not blue. adjustable arm if possible. not ugly. budget around 1500 pesos, give or take. tell me which Mexican brands to look at. I'm going to spend half a saturday on this lamp when I'm back. it's the lamp now. it's the lamp.

one more thing about the visits, while I'm thinking through it. totally unrelated tech-thinking-out-loud while my brain needs a vacation. I've been turning over in my head, since Daniela's perf-doc note, what a clean rewrite of our routing service would look like. don't quote me, this is ten minutes of hammock thinking, but if it were rust:

- axum for the http layer, tokio runtime obviously, sqlx with the postgis feature flag for our spatial queries, redis for the in-memory dispatch cache.
- the route-graph itself has to be loaded once at startup and hot-reloadable on a SIGHUP or admin endpoint. graph mutations are rare. we cache the graph in an Arc<RwLock<RouteGraph>>, readers grab the read lock, writes only happen during the daily ingest.
- pathfinding uses a modified A* with a custom heuristic, currently in python with networkx, which is the part of the service that scales worst. moving that to rust gets us straight-line wins on its own.
- biggest open question is how we serialize the route graph for cache warmup. bincode is fast but not portable. flatbuffers is portable but our team doesn't know it.

that's the back-of-napkin. I'm going to refine this when I'm back at a real keyboard. the real question, when I get there, is whether we do this rewrite at all or whether we squeeze 30% out of the python service first and buy ourselves another year. economic decision, not a technical one.

if you spot a hole in this plan, tell me. otherwise I'm just thinking out loud.

one hole I'll flag for myself: I don't know exactly how axum handles long-poll patterns and we have at least two endpoints that benefit from server-sent events for live-driver-position updates. need to confirm SSE-on-axum is mature. I think it is. need to confirm. another hole: our existing python service has an ML inference call (lightweight gradient-boosted thing for ETA prediction) that's currently called inline. moving to rust either means calling out to a python service for that inference (network overhead) or porting the model to a rust-native inference runtime. neither is ideal. third hole, smaller: our k6 load test framework is set up against the python service's exact endpoints. we'd need to update or rewrite the load tests for the new contract. solvable but real work.

those three holes don't kill the plan. they're sized correctly: each is a one-to-three-day investigation. but I want them on the page when I make the case to Daniela so she sees that I have thought through the not-just-the-happy-path side.

okay. the actual problem. I want to put this on the record because it's been brewing for months and I haven't really written it down clean. and I think writing it out for you helps me sharpen the answer I give Daniela on wednesday.

our main backend service — internally we call it RouteOpt — is the system that takes a list of pending deliveries, the current locations of our 600 last-mile drivers, the time-windows the customers have specified, the road graph of CDMX (and three other cities now), and produces an assignment from drivers to deliveries plus an ordered route per driver. it is the heart of what we do. when it works the company makes money. when it slows down the drivers stand around looking at their phones and the customers complain.

it is currently a python/django monolith. yes, monolith, because we built it in a hurry in 2022 when the company was smaller. it has a django admin panel grafted onto it that operations uses for manual overrides. it has a celery queue for asynchronous batch jobs (mostly the daily route planning runs at 4am). it has a couple of dozen REST endpoints serving the dispatcher app. and it has a python implementation of a modified A* graph search using networkx as the underlying graph library. the original engineers who wrote it are mostly gone — Carla Vela is the only person on the team who was around for the original architecture decisions, and she's about to go on parental leave for five months. so the institutional memory of why decisions were made is leaving the team three weeks from now.

the metrics, as of last month:
- p50 endpoint latency: 80ms (fine)
- p95: 340ms (over our SLA of 300ms — we get yelled at when this number creeps up)
- p99: 2.4s under load (frankly horrifying)
- 4am daily batch job: completes in 38 minutes for CDMX alone, which means we cannot start serving morning routes until ~5am, which is later than ops wants
- memory: ~1.3gb resident on each replica, growing roughly 50mb a week, which is a slow leak nobody has time to chase
- request volume: ~22 million requests/day, peaks at 1200 RPS during morning rush
- 8 replica pods in prod, each on a 4-vCPU 8GB pod

the pain points, in priority order:
1. p95 over SLA. this is the customer-facing one. nobody on ops cares about p99 unless it sustains, but when p95 creeps over 300ms our customer success people start fielding 'why are my deliveries late' calls.
2. that 4am batch job. when we add the next two cities we're rolling out to (Monterrey, Bogotá), we forecast the batch job at ~110 minutes for all of them. that means we can't start serving routes until 6am, which is unacceptable for a logistics company. our customer SLA literally says 'morning routes available by 5am local.'
3. the memory leak. annoying. not blocker. but it forces us to do a rolling restart of the replica set every 5-7 days which adds operational toil and means an engineer needs to be on call for the restart window.
4. the django admin panel is increasingly being used by ops in ways nobody anticipated, and it's becoming a source of accidental data corruption — ops will manually toggle a field through the admin and break invariants that the python ORM doesn't enforce. last month we had a 90-minute incident because someone in ops manually unchecked an 'active' flag on a driver who was mid-route, and the dispatcher app crashed when it tried to render the driver's nullified state. that's a problem of architecture, not django specifically, but django makes it easy to commit those sins.

there are basically three options, and Daniela's perf doc was an attempt to get the team to think clearly about which one to pursue:

(A) iterate on the python/django service. squeeze 30% out of the hot paths. profile the A* implementation, replace networkx with our own thinner graph in pure python, get aggressive about caching (especially the cached-graph thing I'm going to ship this week regardless), kill the django admin in favor of an internal tools app. estimated ~3-4 months of focused engineer time. brings p95 to maybe 230ms and the batch to maybe 28 minutes. doesn't fix the memory leak in any clean way. doesn't help us when we add two more cities. buys us roughly 12 months. then we're back here having the same conversation.

(B) rewrite the pathfinding hot path in rust as a callable extension via pyo3, leave the rest of django alone. estimated ~5-6 months of focused engineer time. brings the A* down by a factor of 8-10x by my back-of-envelope estimates. p95 maybe 120ms. batch maybe 12 minutes. memory leak unchanged. cost: now we have a python/django service with a rust extension which is a real maintenance pattern not everyone on the team can debug. but it's the smallest 'rust' bet. and frankly it's the most credible step on a path to (C) — we'd be writing rust we'd reuse later.

(C) full rewrite. brand new service, rust + axum + tokio + sqlx with the postgis feature, redis cache for hot dispatch state. take everything from scratch. estimated 8-12 months. potentially p95 of 40ms. batch maybe 4-6 minutes. memory leak gone. and a service architected for the next 3 years of growth. we get to redesign the data model around what we actually use, not what django ORM made convenient in 2022. we get to remove the django admin panel and replace it with a controlled internal tools UI that enforces invariants. cost: it's the biggest commitment we'd make as a team this year. we'd need to hire one more rust-experienced engineer or have some of the team upskill. and we'd have to maintain both services side by side for at least the cutover period, which is operational pain. and during that maintenance period there's a non-trivial risk of feature drift between the two services.

Daniela is, I think, leaning toward (C). she has not said so. but she's been asking architectural questions in the doc that only make sense if she's already entertaining (C) seriously — questions about how we'd handle blue/green deployment, questions about runtime memory profiles, questions about whether we'd want to break the service into multiple smaller services or keep it monolithic in rust. those are post-(C) questions. and frankly, when I think about it cold-blooded, I think (C) is the right call too. (A) is fixing a leaky boat. (B) is a duct-tape hybrid we'll regret in 18 months unless we explicitly treat it as a stepping stone toward (C). (C) is the actual answer.

but I don't want to be the loudest voice for (C). there's a senior engineer on our team, Carla Vela, who has more rust experience than anyone (she did a year of rust at her previous company at a payment processor in Madrid), and (C) effectively means making her or me the tech lead of the rewrite. she's eight months pregnant and on parental leave starting in three weeks. so it would land on me. and that's a lot. tech leading a 6-12 month rewrite while also being a recently-promoted senior who is supposed to mentor a junior (Andrés) and be available for cross-team architecture questions is — that's a heavy bag.

there's also a (D) that I want to acknowledge for completeness even though I don't think it's the right call: don't rewrite at all, just throw more pods at the problem. horizontal scaling. it's the cheapest in engineering hours, the most expensive in cloud spend, and it doesn't fix the architectural pain points. but every leadership meeting at every series-B startup eventually has someone propose this. so when I write the answer to Daniela I should mention (D) and explain why we're rejecting it. otherwise the CEO will ask the question in three weeks.

I'm not asking you to decide. I'm asking you to help me think clearly. if Daniela asks me at our 1:1 on wednesday — and she will — 'what do you think we should do?' — what's the most intellectually honest answer I can give that doesn't either oversell rust or undersell the python iteration path? I want my answer to her to be the kind of answer she can use in a leadership meeting without looking like she's just channeling me.

specifically, let me think about how I'd frame it: 'I think we should commit to (C), but begin with the (B) sub-step as a hedge. specifically: spend 6-8 weeks porting the A* hot path to rust as a pyo3 extension. this is a real engineering investment, not a throwaway prototype, and the rust we write here will be reused in the (C) service. by week 6 we'll have shipped a meaningful perf improvement (p95 from 340 to ~150) and we'll have validated whether our team can ship rust to prod. if it goes well, we commit to (C) at week 8 and start the full rewrite. if it goes badly we have a real fallback because we still have the python service. and meanwhile, the cached-graph fix and a couple of other quick wins ship in parallel to keep us within SLA today.' that's the answer I'm sketching. tell me if it has holes.

let me know if I'm missing options. and lay out for me the strongest argument for each of (A) (B) (C) (D) — like, the strongest version of each, presented by an advocate who genuinely believes it. I'll use that to stress-test my own current lean toward 'commit to (C), enter via (B).' I want to walk into Daniela's office on wednesday with a calibrated answer, not a passionate one.

one more thing I want to flag for myself before I close the laptop. whatever we choose, the operational story matters more than the raw perf. the difference between (B) and (C) on perf might be 30-40%. the difference on operational story is bigger. (C) gives us a service we can take pride in operating — clear invariants, clear deployment story, clear blast-radius for changes. (B) leaves us with a hybrid that we will be apologizing for in standups for the next two years. that operational consideration is the thing I most want Daniela to weight in her own thinking, because comp committees and CEOs underweight operational story in favor of perf headlines, and if we let the conversation settle on perf headlines we'll end up with (B) by inertia.

final thought: I want to write all of the above into a one-page memo tonight after Camila is asleep. just for myself. just to make sure I have my own argument crisp. memos for me, not for distribution. that's the intermediate step between 'thinking out loud with you' and 'walking into Daniela's office with a clean recommendation.' three iterations: chat, memo, conversation. that's the loop I trust.
