I don't know what they did internally to make that happen but I'm grateful.

she spent today on the couch. she's reading. she has a stack of like seven novels next to her and she's working through them. one was her mom's. she found it in a box when we got home — a copy of Clarice Lispector's A Hora da Estrela that her mom had marked up with notes in the margins, and Camila has been reading the notes more than the book itself.

the bereavement leave is good. work would not be good for her right now. but I'm worried about week three and four. the silence in the apartment when I'm at the office. she has friends here but most of them are working during the day. and Hugo isn't here yet — he comes in three weeks. I want to make sure she has structure. not pressure-structure, but rhythm-structure. things to do that aren't forced. is there a non-condescending way to suggest she walk over to her firm's office for a coffee with a coworker once a week, just to maintain contact, without it feeling like I'm pushing her to perform recovery? I don't want to get this wrong. she has the ability to read condescension at the molecular level.

the other thing I'm wondering — should I take a couple of half-days myself in week three so the silence isn't full days. work-from-home a morning, lunch with her, work-from-home an afternoon. not every day, but maybe two days a week. would that read as protective or as overbearing? I think it depends entirely on whether she asks for it. I think the right move is: I offer it once, not pressuring, and let her decide. 'hey, just so you know, I can wfh a couple of mornings a week for the next few weeks if you want. zero pressure either way, just want you to know it's available.' that feels right. I'll write that on a sticky note and stick it on the fridge so she sees it during the day rather than as a heavy in-person ask.

Andrés sent me his profiling output from the staging RouteOpt service. 41% of CPU time is spent in networkx graph traversal. 19% in serializing django ORM responses. 8% in our middleware logging hook. 'wow' is a verbal tic I rarely use but. wow. that 41% is exactly the budget I needed to see to make the pyo3 case to Daniela. graph traversal is the biggest single line item in the profile and it is also the cleanest thing to port to rust because it's pure compute on a self-contained data structure. this is the universe handing me an answer.

follow up on the profile. so 41% in networkx is way more than I expected. I knew networkx was slow but I assumed maybe 20%. 41% means we are spending almost half the request building and querying a graph object every single time. I don't think we even need to be doing the build per request — the road graph for a city doesn't change minute to minute. we should be loading it once at process startup and caching it in memory.

actually let me reread our code. there's a chance we're doing something dumb like reconstructing the graph from rows on every API hit. that would be bad. that would also explain the memory growth — if we're rebuilding it every time we have a window for objects to leak through circular references that python's gc has to special-case.

okay I'll dig into that tomorrow morning. wanted to write it out before I forgot.

separately — running mental math. if we do option (A) (iterate on python), this single change of caching the graph could already get us 25% of the way to our perf goals. that's interesting. that doesn't change the long-term answer (we still want option (C) for the next two cities) but it does mean we could front-load a quick win in the next two weeks while we scope (C). I'll propose that to Daniela on wednesday.

the sequencing I want to suggest: ship the cache fix first as a low-risk single-PR change behind a feature flag. observe in staging for 48 hours with our regular load profile. roll out to prod 10/50/100 over a week. that buys us our 25% win without any architecture decision being made about the larger rewrite. then in parallel I scope option (C) properly with a written design doc that has the four or five questions I asked Daniela in the original thread answered concretely, and we make the rewrite call with cooler heads in two to three weeks. that's the responsible plan. fast win, slow decision.

the other thing I want to do — and this is partly career-shaped because of the promo timing — is write up the cache-fix discovery as a short tech-blog-internal post for our engineering wiki. not because I'm trying to look good for the comp committee (though it would not hurt). because the lesson is genuinely interesting: a three-year-old TODO sitting in a hot path was 25% of our latency. the takeaway for the team is 'old TODOs in hot paths are technical debt with compounding interest.' that's worth twenty minutes of writing.

wednesday. 1:1 with Daniela. forty-five minutes. she pulled me into the small meeting room with the green wall, the one we call 'the lime.' I had a coffee. she had a cortado. she kicked off the way she always does, with: 'so. how are you. real version, not the one you tell other people.' that's the line she opens every 1:1 with. she has been opening 1:1s with that line since I joined Brújula. it doesn't sound rehearsed when she says it because she actually waits for the real answer.

so I gave her the real version. I told her about the funeral, about Hugo, about the once-a-month visits starting in three weeks. about Camila being on bereavement leave through the end of the month. about my own residual jet-lag and the fact that I am sleeping like a man who has been asked to sleep next to an unknown sound. she didn't try to fix anything. she just listened, and at one point she put her hand briefly on the table near mine, not on my hand, just near it, in the kind of solidarity gesture that doesn't ask anything of you. she has lost a parent herself — her father died a couple of years ago, before I joined the company — so she knows the shape of this from the inside. she didn't bring up her own loss. that's also one of her tells. she does not perform empathy. she just radiates it.

then she said: 'okay. so. there's a thing I want to talk to you about and I want to be respectful of where you are this week. tell me if it's the wrong time and we can push it.' I said no, please, work is actually a relief, please go ahead. and I meant it. I had been waiting for forty-eight hours for the news that she signaled in her cryptic 'we'll talk about other things' message from São Paulo, and the suspense had become its own kind of mental weight.

she said: 'end of year promotion cycle is open. I'm putting you up for senior. it's not a slam dunk but I think it's a strong case and I want to write it.'

I sat there for about ten seconds without saying anything. she let me sit. she's good at that. she has the rare manager skill of letting silence do work. most managers fill silence with reassurance or with caveats. she just lets it sit.

I've been at Brújula since late 2023. I came in as a mid-level. about fifteen months in. the typical promo to senior at Brújula is twenty-four to thirty months in, so this is a slightly accelerated case. she said she'd been thinking about it for a couple of months and the postgres outage we had a few weeks before São Paulo (the four-hour one in May) — where I'd written the post-mortem and led a lot of the recovery — was the thing that pushed her over the edge. she'd watched how I handled it. she'd watched how I wrote the blame-free post-mortem. she said 'that document is what a senior engineer writes. I want to make it official.' her words. she repeated 'I want to make it official' twice during the meeting, which is a Daniela tell — when she repeats a sentence in a meeting, it's because she's already had this conversation in her head a number of times and she's done committing to it.

she walked me through the case she's writing. she has it in a draft google doc. she shared it with me on the spot. it cites three specific things: the post-mortem (with three excerpts from the doc, including the part where I argued for a less-blameful framing of an ops mistake), my mentorship of Andrés over the last four months (which I had not even thought of as mentorship, just as 'helping the junior get unstuck' — apparently Andrés had told her in his last 1:1 that the reason he hasn't quit is that I made him feel like he wasn't drowning), and the architectural input I'd given on the routing rewrite scoping (which was, like, a couple of slack comments and one hour-long whiteboard session in early May). she'd also added a section on cross-team work — apparently I'd given a clean architectural review to the platform team back in March that they'd referenced in their own quarterly retro, which I had completely forgotten about.

she also told me — this is the part that matters and that I'm chewing on tonight — that she expects me to be the tech lead on the rust rewrite if we go that direction. she said 'I'm scoping it as part of the senior-level case. the senior promotion comes with leading the rewrite. those are two parts of the same package.' she did not present this as a take-it-or-leave-it. she presented it as an explanation of why the case is strong. but the implication was clear: the senior level at Brújula is the level where you start leading projects of this size, and the rewrite is the project that justifies the senior title. without the rewrite the case is harder to make. with the rewrite it becomes obvious.

okay. so. the package. seventeen percent salary bump if it goes through. tech lead role on a major rewrite that will define the next year of my work life. and the timing is — well. it is what it is. it's right now. it's three days after I came back from a funeral.

I said yes. of course I said yes. I'd be a fool to say no. it's the right move for my career, it's the right move for the team, it's a vote of confidence from a manager who I trust deeply. I told Daniela that I was excited and grateful and that I needed maybe a week to be in proper ramp-up shape because of the family stuff. she said 'take two weeks. ramp up at your pace. the comp committee meets at end of next month, that's our deadline for the case, and we'll be fine.' and then she added something that I don't think I've ever heard a manager say before — she said 'and Seb, this is going to sound counterintuitive, but I want you to know — I am not going to penalize you in the writing for being slow this week. I want you to write the rewrite case from a place of recovered energy, not from a place of pretending you have energy you don't. so when you do the architectural document for the project, do it next week or the week after, when you've slept properly. that document is going to be the spine of the case and I want it to be your best thinking, not your jet-lagged thinking.' that is calibrated management. she handed me a permission to be slow without making the slowness about my grief. she made it about the quality of the document. that is the move of a manager who knows how to coach a senior.

but here's the part I don't know how to feel about. I walked out of that meeting and I had this moment in the hallway where I was just standing there next to the water cooler grinning to myself like an idiot. like a real grin. for about thirty seconds I was the happiest I've been in three weeks. and then I caught myself grinning and I felt this wave of guilt because Camila is at home reading her mom's marked-up copy of Clarice Lispector and I am at the office getting promoted to senior. those two things should not be co-occurring. those two things should be six months apart. but they're not. they are the same week. that's the texture of life — it doesn't separate the joys from the griefs into separate weeks. they show up on the same Wednesday afternoon and you have to hold both.

the ratio of joy in our apartment right now is — let me try to get this right — at most fifteen percent should be mine, at most. she should hold the rest. and yet I just took on something that is going to add maybe twenty percent more joy to my side of the ledger. I'm going to come home tonight and I'm going to have to decide how to tell her. and if I get it wrong, I will introduce a tiny crack into the apartment that is going to need months to heal. so I want to get it right.

that's the thing I want to ask you about. how do I tell my partner — who buried her mother nine days ago — that I got promoted today, in a way that is honest and shares the news but doesn't ask her to perform celebration that she doesn't have? she will perform if I let her. she is the kind of person who will throw her arms around me and say 'amor I'm so proud of you' and mean it and also be hollowed out doing it. I don't want her to spend any of her thin energy reserves making me feel celebrated. that's not what I need from her this week.

I was thinking I could tell her at dinner, very low-key, like 'hey by the way Daniela mentioned today she's putting me up for senior, comp committee meets end of next month, no pressure on either of us either way, just wanted you to know.' that's the spirit. understated. real. doesn't make it a moment. lets her absorb it as information rather than as an event. she can come back to it tomorrow if she wants. or next week. or never. the news is on the table without expectation.

but Camila will know. she'll see through the understated framing immediately. she will know that this is a big deal for me. she will say 'amor. that's huge.' and she'll mean it. and then she'll cry, possibly. for entirely separate reasons, possibly braided together.

is there a way to do this where I don't make her cry? or do I let her cry and not flinch from it? I think the answer might be the second one. but I want to hear how you'd frame it. specifically — give me a few openings. give me three different framings, each one a few sentences long, that I can pick from depending on her mood when I get home. one for if she's reading on the couch. one for if she's already in bed. one for if she's at the kitchen table working on something. tone calibration matters here. I want options.

for what it's worth — Daniela is the one who put me up for this. I want to remember her name on the record. Daniela Otero. she's been my manager for a year and three months. she is one of the few managers I've ever had who I would walk into a fire for. she has the kind of management style that makes you better at your job by osmosis. you are around her for six months and you start writing better tickets, running better meetings, giving better feedback, simply because you have absorbed her cadence. if she leaves Brújula, I will leave too within six months. that is not on the table right now but I want to write it down because I will not be honest with myself about it later. she's that good. and the senior promotion is going to be partly because of who I am and partly because of who she is, and I will never quite know the proportions, but I will not pretend that the second factor is small.

I told her. tonight. at dinner. cooked some lentil soup that I made up as I went, served it with a piece of bread Doña Marta had brought down earlier in the week. I waited until we were halfway through the soup. then I said the thing about Daniela putting me up for senior, exactly the way I'd planned, low-key, no pressure either way.

she put her spoon down. she looked at me for a long second. then she got up, walked around the table, and hugged me from the side, my face pressed into her ribs, and she said 'amor.' just that. amor.

then she sat back down, picked up her spoon, took another sip of soup, and said 'you have to call your mom tonight. she's going to be insufferable. she's going to want to throw a party in Guadalajara.'

she didn't cry. she didn't perform celebration. she made a joke about my mom. it was perfect. she's perfect.

I called my mom after dinner. she answered on the first ring. she said 'mijo' in this tone that knew immediately something was up — moms have radar — and I told her and she screamed 'aaaaaay HIJO' and I had to hold the phone away from my ear. she put my dad on. my dad said 'felicidades, mijo, ya te lo merecías' and asked me three specific questions about what 'senior' means in pesos, which is the most charming thing he could have done. fifteen minutes on the phone with them and then I came back to the couch where Camila was reading and put my head in her lap and she stroked my hair and we sat like that until I fell asleep on her thigh.

she woke me at midnight. led me to bed. that's the day.

I keep going back to her face when she put the spoon down. there was a half-second where I could see her two reactions racing each other — the grief reflex and the joy reflex — and the joy one won, just barely, and that was a small grace I want to mark. she could have made it about her, about the contrast, about how complicated joy feels when your mother is three weeks dead. she didn't. she made it about me. she made it about us. she chose to be present with my good news rather than to be present with her sadness in that moment, and she did it cleanly, no performance. that is the kind of partner she is. five years in and I am still surprised by her generosity in moments like that. I want to remember it. I want to be that kind of partner back when her good news comes.

confirmed: yes, we ARE rebuilding the networkx graph on every request. it's in a function called `get_route_graph()` in routes/views.py and it's called from like fourteen places. someone wrote a TODO three years ago saying 'cache this' and nobody did. fixing it now. lru_cache with a maxsize tied to active CDMX subgraphs, invalidation hook on the road-update event bus. couple of hours of work, massive payoff, blast radius small if I gate it behind a feature flag for a week.

ok pushed a fix. cached the graph at module load with a lazy initializer. wrote a small invalidation hook that gets triggered when the daily ingest job updates the road graph rows. local benchmark shows the new code path is roughly 4x faster on the median request and like 9x faster on the slow tail. didn't expect 9x. that suggests the graph rebuild was the dominant cost on the worst requests, which makes sense — the rebuilds were doing a O(n^2) thing under the hood that I now skip entirely.

asked Daniela if I can deploy to staging tomorrow morning and run the k6 perf suite against it to confirm. she said yes. if the numbers match my local bench, this single change will move our p95 from 340 to maybe 220. that wouldn't fix the long-term problem but it'd take us out of SLA-violation territory in the short term.

that would also genuinely strengthen Daniela's senior promo case for me. like, the timing is fine. it's not why I did it, I did it because I saw the bug, but it's a nice instrumental side effect.

I'm typing this from the couch. Camila is across the room reading. Nube would be on my feet if we had a Nube. (we don't. we keep talking about getting a dog. someday.)

one thing I should write down so I don't forget — the cache invalidation hook is the part that scared me when I was writing it, because cache invalidation is one of the two hard things in computer science. but our case is gentle: the road graph changes maybe once a day during the ingest job, the ingest job is a single batch process I can hook into directly, and a stale graph for a few extra seconds during the swap-over is not a correctness issue (it's a freshness issue). so I went with a single global cache pointer, atomic swap on ingest completion, no read locks needed because the readers see the new pointer or the old pointer and both are valid graphs. simple. the simplicity is the right call. I want to remember that I resisted the urge to over-engineer this with a versioned cache or a multi-region invalidation pattern. the simple version shipped tonight. the over-engineered version would still be in design review.

it's saturday. ten days since we got back. fourteen days since the funeral. I want to write down where Camila is, where I am, where the apartment is, before I lose track. the days are starting to blur into each other and I want a snapshot. partly because I want to be able to read this back in a month and see how the picture has shifted. partly because writing it makes the world legible to me when it doesn't always feel legible inside.

Camila. she's reading a lot. she's started cooking again — last night she made arroz à grega which is a Brazilian dish her mom used to make for her when she was sick. yellow rice with raisins and ham (she made me a vegetarian version with mushrooms instead of ham, on a separate pan, she insisted). she stood over the stove for forty minutes stirring with this distant focus, like she was trying to taste her mother through the dish. she's been crying about once a day, usually in the late afternoon, usually triggered by some small thing — yesterday she cried because she saw a woman on the street wearing a coat in the same shade of camel as one of her mom's coats. then she stopped crying after about ten minutes and went back to whatever she was doing. the day before she cried because the cleaning lady moved a vase to dust under it and put it back two centimeters off-center, which Bea would have noticed and corrected, and Camila stood in the living room and put her hand on the vase and burst into tears for forty seconds, then wiped her face with the back of her wrist and walked into the kitchen.

she has good fifteen-minute stretches now where she seems entirely herself. ten days ago there were no such stretches. that's progress, I think. progress is the wrong word. that's something. the wrongness of the world is starting to be punctuated by stretches of okayness, instead of the other way around. that's the metric I'm tracking. before São Paulo, her baseline was 100% Camila and 0% wrongness. last week it was 0% Camila and 100% wrongness. now it's maybe 35% Camila and 65% wrongness, with the Camila stretches getting longer over time. somewhere there's a curve that flattens out at maybe 80%, where she is the post-Bea Camila and that's just who she is now. I think we get there in eight or twelve months. I don't have evidence for that timeline. I just have a feeling.

she's been talking to Hugo on the phone every day. they have a 9pm call ritual. he calls. she answers. they talk for forty-five minutes to an hour. some nights she comes back from the call with red eyes. some nights she comes back laughing about a story he told. some nights she comes back blank. I leave them alone. I don't ask what they talked about unless she wants to share. tonight she came back from the call and said 'papai is doing the laundry. he had never done the laundry. mama always did the laundry. he just told me he washed a wool sweater on hot and shrunk it down to doll-size.' she was laughing. she said it like a delighted seven-year-old reporting on her dad's incompetence. and then she said, quieter, 'I should have visited more. I should have taught him how to use the washing machine.' and then she went into the bathroom and was in there for ten minutes. that's the rhythm of it. one moment of laughter, one moment of regret. they take turns.

she's not back at work. she has another two weeks of bereavement leave officially. I expect she'll go back the second week, maybe four hours a day. the architecture firm has been saintly so far. her director Marina sent her a handwritten card last tuesday — actual paper, hand-delivered to our apartment by a courier — that said 'we will be here when you're ready. take your time. don't perform readiness.' direct quote. that's a sentence to put on a t-shirt. that's how you write a card to a grieving employee. Marina has my respect for life now.

me. I am — okay. I notice that I keep using the phrase 'i'm fine' when people from Brújula ask, and 'i'm okay' when my parents ask, and I have not really articulated what's actually going on under there because I've been running so hard on the work side. the work side is good. the senior promo is in motion. the routeopt fix landed in staging this morning and the numbers held — p95 went from 340 to 215. Daniela slacked me 'absolute hero' which from her is a thirteen-stage standing ovation. the rust prototype is taking shape. I have momentum at work. that helps. it gives me a place to put my hands during the days that would otherwise be too quiet.

but underneath that, I am tired in a way I haven't been in years. like, my body is tired. my back has been catching at me when I sit too long, especially on the work-from-home days, and I think that's leftover stress from carrying the casket honestly. my shoulders have been knotted since São Paulo. I went to Atlas Box twice this week — Wednesday and Friday — and the Wednesday class wrecked me. I had to sit out the last two rounds. Profe Esteban looked at me and just said 'you good, mijo' which from him means 'you don't look good, mijo' and I said 'family stuff, profe, will be back to normal next week' and he nodded and didn't push. on Friday I made it through all eight rounds but my form was sloppy in the last two and Pablo, my regular sparring partner, caught me with a left hook to the body in the last round that knocked the wind out of me for a full thirty seconds. that's not him being aggressive. that's me being slow. when you're slow you eat shots that you would normally slip. I'm noting it. I expect to be slow for another two weeks and I should not spar full-contact during that period.

I think what I'm processing — and this is where I'm going to be a little floppy — is that this past two weeks have permanently rearranged my sense of how stable my life is. I had this baseline assumption, before all this, that the people in my life who are alive will stay alive for a long time. that's a stupid assumption. it's stupid the way assuming the floor is solid is stupid. you don't think about it until something gives way. but I had it. I was operating on it. I had built an entire architecture of plans on it. I think most people in their early thirties operate on this assumption without noticing. it's invisible until you bury someone and then you can see it everywhere — in your calendar, in your savings allocation, in your career timeline, in the way you put off phone calls.

the plans I had: keep working at Brújula for two more years, get the senior promotion, save 30% of my income, eventually buy an apartment in Roma Norte or Condesa, propose to Camila in maybe eighteen months, get married in maybe two and a half years, kids in maybe four years if we wanted them. somewhere in that timeline my parents and Camila's parents would all attend our wedding. I had built that timeline in my head for five years and it never occurred to me that one of those four parents wouldn't make it. that future-list is now a different list. now it's: my parents and Camila's father would all attend our wedding. one parent is missing. that's the new fact. and the wedding doesn't even feel like the right word for it now — it feels like a gathering with a hole in it.

now Bea is gone. and I'm staring at the rest of the timeline going — when's the next one. statistically, my parents are 62 and 66. my dad has had a triple bypass. my mom is healthy but she's a nurse in a country with bad cancer screening rates. Hugo Ferreira sr — Camila's dad — has angina, mild but real. Mama Lupita is already gone. I have not seen my parents in person since may. I last saw them at my dad's birthday in Guadalajara. that's four months. Camila hasn't seen her dad alone since the funeral but he comes in two weeks. four months without seeing my parents is, in this new framework, an absurd amount of time. I would not have said that two weeks ago. I would have said 'I see them every couple of months, that's plenty.' now I want to drive to Guadalajara tomorrow. I won't. but I want to.

I keep thinking — what does it mean to be thirty-two years old and to suddenly understand that my parents are mortal? I knew it intellectually before. I did not feel it. now I feel it. and the feeling is — uncomfortable. it's a low background dread that I cannot turn off. I want to fly to Guadalajara every weekend now. that's not realistic. that's not what they want either, they'd be confused if I started showing up every weekend. but the urge is there. I want to call my mom every night. I want my dad to teach me chess properly. I want Mama Lupita back, which is impossible. I want time to slow down. I want to be five years old in my parents' kitchen in Guadalajara again with my abuela telling me to wait for the chocolate caliente to cool. I want a thousand things I cannot have.

I lit a candle for Lupita this morning. it wasn't her birthday. I just lit a candle. on the kitchen table. Camila walked in and saw it and said nothing, just nodded and went on with her morning. I think she understood. when you live with someone whose mother just died, candles for the dead require no explanation. she sat down at the table opposite me with her own coffee and we just sat there for fifteen minutes, the candle between us, neither of us saying anything. that's a kind of communion. that's lapsed-catholic communion, which is the only kind I can perform.

I am still a lapsed Catholic. I am not converting back. but I notice that the rituals are useful in a way they weren't before. lighting a candle. saying a name out loud. these are tools the church developed because humans are wired this way. I don't have to believe in saints to use the candle. when I lit it I said 'abuela, te quiero, perdóname' under my breath. that 'perdóname' is the part I do not put down. it's the regret about not visiting her in September 2019, the regret that has not gone away in six years, the regret that is going to follow me for the rest of my life as a small dark stone in my pocket. lighting the candle didn't dissolve it. but it reminded me to keep carrying it. that's the thing rituals do. they don't fix grief. they make a place for grief to live so it doesn't possess you.

oh — language thing. since we got back, I've been keeping up the Portuguese. duolingo daily, but more importantly Camila and I are speaking maybe 30% Portuguese around the house now. I think it started because she keeps having Portuguese phone calls with her dad and the household sound has shifted, and now we drift into Portuguese for short stretches. her family WhatsApps me directly now too — uncle Joaquim sent me a video yesterday of his grandchildren and a paragraph of context, all in Portuguese. tia Marta sent me a recipe for arroz de forno (with the mexican adaptation, no pork, she made the modification herself) yesterday morning. I'm in their family group chat now. they have added me. before the funeral I was on the periphery of that group chat. now I'm in it. that's a status promotion that funerals do — they move you up a circle. it's good for me. before the funeral my Portuguese was theoretical B1, like, I could pass a B1 test but I couldn't have a real-life argument with someone in it. now my Portuguese is real-life B1. there's a phase change in there. I'm noticing that I'm starting to think small thoughts in Portuguese, the way you do when a language is moving from external to internal. yesterday I had an internal monologue at the metro turnstile that was in Portuguese for about three sentences. I noticed it because I caught myself thinking 'caramba' instead of 'ay no' when the turnstile rejected my card. small. but real.

I'm rambling. it's saturday. Camila is making coffee and I can smell it. I think I'll close this and go drink it with her. tomorrow there's going to be ten more things to deal with — Hugo's room is still half-done, I have two prs in code review I have to address, Daniela wants a draft of the rust architecture doc by tuesday, and we've got dinner at Joaquín's place on sunday with the friend group which will be the first time we've seen people other than Doña Marta since we got back. for now, just — coffee. one cup. with my partner. on a saturday morning that, for the first time in two weeks, feels like it might be a regular saturday morning. that's enough.

the airline finally got back to us. they lost Camila's checked bag on the way back from São Paulo. nine days later. they say it'll be delivered to the apartment within 48 hours. the bag has — among other things — her mom's address book in it. she lit up when she heard. fingers crossed. she has been describing the address book to me as if it was a saint's relic. her mom's handwriting on every page. phone numbers from the eighties. people no one has called in fifteen years. I want that bag delivered. I want it delivered yesterday. I will be at the apartment all day waiting.

scoping meeting today for the routing rewrite. four of us in a room — Daniela, me, Carla Vela (going on parental leave in two weeks), Andrés. we walked through options A/B/C from my earlier write-up. Daniela framed it as a decision discussion, not a brainstorm.

general consensus: option (C). full rewrite in rust. nobody fought for (A) honestly. Carla advocated for a slightly different (B) where we'd port the A* hot path to rust as a pyo3 extension first as a stepping stone, then do the rest in rust over the following six months. that's a hybrid. it has the advantage of getting some perf wins fast without a full cutover.

Daniela said she'd think about it. I think she's going to land on something between (B) and (C) — something like 'start with the A* port, but commit fully to (C) by month four if we're not seeing the right metrics.' a graduated commitment.

she asked me if I'd be tech lead. in front of the team. I said yes, on the conditions that (a) Andrés gets to be on the project full-time and grow on it, and (b) we don't kill the python service for at least three months past the rust go-live, to give us a real fallback. she said yes to both. handshake, basically.

tech lead. that's a thing I haven't been before. I've led technical decisions, I've mentored juniors, I've owned services. but tech-leading a multi-quarter rewrite is a different beast. I'm equal parts excited and slightly terrified. mostly excited. terrified is the smaller part.

the stretch I'm sitting with — the things that distinguish 'senior IC who leads technical decisions' from 'tech lead' as I understand the role: weekly written status updates to leadership, owning the decisions about what's in scope and what isn't, advocating for engineering time in roadmap meetings I'm not currently in, mentoring Andrés in a structured way rather than ad-hoc, code review where I have to say no to colleagues' approaches sometimes, hiring conversations once we have headcount approved, and the quietly-political work of keeping Daniela appropriately informed without flooding her with detail. that last one is the one I'm least sure about. I know how to ship code. I know how to mentor. I'm less sure how to communicate up effectively. that's the place I'll need to ask for explicit feedback from Daniela in our 1:1s for the next few months.

I want to do a kind of state-of-the-union for myself. it's sunday night. exactly two weeks since we got home from São Paulo. three weeks since the funeral. I'm sitting at our kitchen table with a yerba mate and my mate cup, the one Mama Lupita gave me, and a small cedar candle Camila put on the table earlier — not for any specific reason, she just lit it after dinner — and I want to write down everything that's going on so I can read it back in a month and see how the picture has shifted.

state of the union, Sebastián edition, two weeks post-funeral.

WORK. promotion to senior is in motion. Daniela is writing the case. comp committee meets in roughly five weeks. she's signaled high confidence. I'm now tech lead on the routeopt rust migration, which is a 6-12 month thing. we've decided on a hybrid (B)/(C) approach: port the A* hot path to rust as a pyo3 extension first (~2 months), then full rewrite in rust + axum + tokio + sqlx (~6 months after that). team will be me, Andrés (junior, four months in), and Carla Vela when she's back from parental leave (in approximately 4-5 months). until then, basically me and Andrés. Daniela will be in air cover. that's a lot. I'm going to need to be deliberate about how I sequence the early weeks. the cached-graph fix shipped to staging on friday and held in production this morning. p95 is now around 215ms. that's our floor for the rest of the migration. anything below that is upside.

FAMILY. parents are in Guadalajara. they're fine. mom is overflowing with promo joy on my behalf. dad is being his proud-but-quiet self, sent me a message yesterday that just said 'estoy orgulloso, mijo. te quiero.' three words. he doesn't usually do that. it landed. I'll see them in person next month, I think — Camila and I are going to fly out for a long weekend so they can hug her and feed her and tell her in person they love her. she has not seen them since the funeral. she wants to go.

brother Mateo is in Tijuana, doing fine. we have not spoken since the funeral, just text messages. I should call him this week.

Mama Lupita is gone but very present. I have lit two candles for her in the past week, more than I usually do in a year. Camila's mom is gone and very present. there's a small framed photograph of Beatriz on our living room shelf that Camila put there last weekend. I look at it every day on my way to make coffee. I say good morning to her in my head. I do not believe in ghosts. I do believe in the work that the photograph is doing for Camila and now for me too.

Camila's dad Hugo is in São Paulo, alone. he calls every night at 9. he flies to CDMX in less than two weeks for his first monthly visit. I have done about 40% of the work to make our spare room a real guest room — bought the lamp (a small ceramic thing from Liverpool, the salesperson and I had a twenty-minute conversation about which one was the right one, in the end we picked one with a brushed brass shade), bought new sheets, cleared out the junk. still need: better mattress, blackout curtain, a small bookshelf for him to put his books on. budget for the rest is fine.

CAMILA. she is at week two of bereavement leave. she will go back to work, probably part-time, in a week or two. she is reading a lot. she is talking to her dad daily. she has cried at least once each day for fourteen days but the crying has shifted in character — it's shorter, more located, less totalizing. she has good stretches. she is making meals again. she has made jokes. last night she made me snort-laugh at a joke about Pope Francis that I cannot repeat in writing because it is mildly heretical. that was the first time I had laughed in two weeks at something that wasn't an internal Brújula slack joke.

she and I are okay. we are tired. we sleep poorly some nights. we have not had sex since the night before the funeral. that has not been a problem so far but I am aware of it as a small thing on the runway. I am not going to bring it up. when she is ready she will be ready. or we will talk about it together, eventually, and we will be fine.

MY BODY. tired. shoulders are knotted. lower back has been catching, especially on the days I work from the kitchen table. I went to kickboxing on Monday, Wednesday, Friday this week, and Friday I was actually back to my normal speed and conditioning. that's good. Profe Esteban gave me a fist bump on the way out which is high praise. I am doing my mobility homework — hip openers, thoracic spine rotations — every other morning. body is repairing.

MY HEAD. that's where this gets harder. let me try.

I'm in this state where work is going extremely well, my partner is grieving but functional, my parents are healthy and proud, and I am — what's the word — I'm uneasy. underneath everything I am uneasy. I keep waiting for the next bad thing. like, my brain has decided that bad things come in threes (a stupid superstition I do not actually endorse but apparently have absorbed) and we have only had one. I keep checking my phone for the next call. mom's phone rings, I flinch. dad calls, I flinch. unknown number, I flinch. I'm not sleeping deeply. I wake up at 4 or 5am sometimes and lie there until the alarm.

I'm not depressed. I want to be careful about the language. I am not depressed. depression for me looks like loss of pleasure and I still very much enjoy my mate, my morning coffee, my evening at the gym, the texture of code working. so I'm not depressed. I am, what, hyper-vigilant. that's the word from the trauma literature. I am hyper-vigilant. and I think that's a normal response to suddenly having seen, up close, the way a family member can vanish in a week.

I've thought about therapy. for me. not for Camila. she's already been seeing someone. for me — I haven't been to therapy ever, except for one couples session a few years ago with a previous girlfriend that didn't really count. I would consider it. I have a friend, Pablo, who has been seeing someone for two years and swears by it. he's a mechanical engineer, very practical guy, and he says therapy is the most engineering-brain-compatible thing he's ever done. that argument lands with me.

I haven't done it yet. I might. I'll think about it for another month and see.

for now my coping mechanisms are: kickboxing, yerba mate, cooking dinner most nights for Camila, calling my mom every other day, lighting candles when I feel like it, reading borges before bed (I'm rereading 'el aleph,' which is the wrong choice for hyper-vigilance, but it's what I'm in the mood for), and writing here at 11pm on sundays when the apartment is quiet.

I think this is fine for now. I think I am doing approximately the right things. I think Camila is doing approximately the right things. I think the next month will be a slow climb back to a more normal version of our life, with the addition of Hugo's monthly visits and the addition of my new tech-lead role and the addition of, presumably, the senior promotion landing.

addendum because I'm not done and the candle is still going. RECENT WINS, in no particular order: I made a vegetarian feijoada last tuesday that Camila said was the best thing I've cooked since we moved in together. I shipped the cached-graph optimization without a single rollback. I had my first full eight-hour sleep on saturday night for the first time since we got back from São Paulo. I called my brother Mateo on thursday — I lied earlier, we did talk, just briefly, he sent me a voice note about how he and his wife Lucía are coming to CDMX for new year's and I should make sure I'm not traveling. I responded yes and meant it.

RECENT LOSSES, also in no particular order: I forgot Marina's birthday by two days. she works at our local panadería and gave us a card after the funeral and I had told her in september I'd remember. I didn't. I dropped off flowers on saturday with a note apologizing and she was kind about it but I felt like garbage. I have been short with Andrés twice this week in standup over things that did not warrant being short. I need to apologize on monday. I bought a new pair of running shoes that I have not worn yet because every time I think about going for a run I think about the fact that I haven't run in six months and the shoes feel like an accusation in their box by the door.

OPEN QUESTIONS that I'm carrying around. first: are Camila and I going to need couples therapy at some point in the next few months, separate from her individual work and my potential individual work, or is this a thing we navigate ourselves. I genuinely do not know. I lean toward 'we navigate ourselves for now and reassess in three months,' but I want to write down that I noticed the question. second: when Hugo comes for his first monthly visit, what's the right rhythm for the four days he's here. do I take time off work entirely. do I work normal hours and let him and Camila have the days together. somewhere between. I have not asked Camila yet what she'd prefer. I will ask her this week, before he books the flights firmly.

third open question, the deepest one: am I going to look back at this period of my life — the funeral, the migration, the promotion, Hugo's visits, Camila's recovery — and recognize it as the thing that aged me from a guy in his early thirties into a fully adult person. I think I will. I think this is the season where I stop being a slightly older version of the kid who ran kicking through the courtyard of his abuela's house and start being a man whose abuela is gone, whose mother-in-law is gone, who has people who depend on him. that is fine. that is the deal. I just want to mark that I noticed the door swinging closed behind me.

that's my state of the union. I'll close this and go to bed and probably wake up at 4am again. but for now — yerba mate, candle, kitchen, Camila asleep down the hall, and I am, all things considered, here. I am here. that's enough for tonight.

spent today setting up the rust skeleton for the A* port. cargo workspace, two crates — `routeopt-core` (the algorithm) and `routeopt-py` (the pyo3 bindings). chose tokio 1.x and serde, no axum yet (we're not exposing http from the rust side, we're just shipping a python extension for now).

first snag — I forgot how the maturin build pipeline interacts with our internal docker images. our prod container is python 3.11 on debian slim. need to confirm maturin can produce a manylinux wheel that's compatible with our exact base image. spent an hour on this and concluded: yes, but we need to add a manylinux build step to our CI which the platform team will need to approve.

next snag — pyo3's GIL handling. our python service is sync django, no async. so the pyo3 entry points need to release the GIL while doing the heavy A* work or python's responsiveness in the django process will tank. that's `py.allow_threads(|| { ... })` which I knew but had to remember. easy.

biggest open question is how we represent the road graph in rust for the algorithm. I want to avoid using a generic graph library because we don't need most of the features — we just need an adjacency list with weighted edges, support for batched updates, and very fast neighbor enumeration. I'm thinking a custom struct: `RoadGraph { nodes: Vec<Node>, edges: Vec<Edge>, adjacency: Vec<Vec<EdgeIndex>> }` where `EdgeIndex` is a newtype around u32. cache-friendly. allocation-free in the hot path. I'll prototype this tomorrow and see if it actually wins over petgraph.

posting this here mostly to think out loud. tomorrow morning is hot path prototyping. wednesday is profile + perf comparison vs python. thursday is integrating the python wrapper. friday is staging deploy. that's an aggressive week and I might not hit all of it but the shape is right.

the other thing I'm going to do tomorrow is set up `criterion` benchmarks for the rust crate from day one. I want every PR I merge to have a benchmark number attached. if I don't put that habit in place at the start of the project, we'll be a month in before we add benchmarks and we'll have lost the historical baseline. cheap to do now, expensive to retrofit. classic infrastructure-debt avoidance.

and a property test or two while we're at it. specifically: a property test that says 'for any random graph with N nodes and M edges, the rust A* and the python A* return the same shortest-path cost.' if that property holds across, say, 10000 random graphs, we have very high confidence we have not introduced an algorithmic bug in the port. that's the testing pattern Carla uses on her stuff and it's the right one for an algorithmic port.

lost bag arrived. courier dropped it off at 7pm. her mom's address book was in it, intact. Camila sat with it on the couch for half an hour, just turning the pages. she found her own name in her mom's handwriting under the C's. she didn't cry. she just put a finger on the page. then she got up and went to the kitchen and made me a cup of mate exactly the way I like it without asking, which she has not done in three weeks. small reciprocity. I drank it slowly.

wednesday. day three of the rust skeleton work. perf comparison day. I'm typing this from café Cardinal in Roma Norte because the apartment has been a little stuffy with Camila in it all day and I needed to get out. I have a long black, my laptop, my notebook, and the cat that lives at Cardinal is sitting on the chair across from me staring at me with what I am choosing to interpret as encouragement.

let me walk you through what I did today and what we're staring at, because I want to think it through and writing it out helps.

the A* port. I built a rust prototype that takes the same input as our python A* — a serialized graph plus a list of (origin, destination, time_window) tuples — and returns the same shape of output. wired it up as a pyo3 extension. ran our existing test suite against it. all 47 tests pass on first try, which is suspicious so I added five more edge-case tests, all pass. the algorithm is doing the same thing.

perf:
- python implementation, batch of 1000 (origin, destination) pairs against the CDMX road graph (~180k nodes, ~600k edges): 4.8 seconds
- rust implementation, same batch: 0.31 seconds
- ratio: ~15.5x faster.

that's better than I expected. I was estimating 8-10x. some of the win is rust being rust. some of the win is that the python implementation has a layer of dict lookups for adjacency that the rust version replaces with direct vec indexing. the cache locality on the rust side is dramatically better. the algorithm itself is identical.

what does this mean for our service end-to-end? our typical request invokes the A* maybe twice — once for a single-driver route, sometimes more for the dispatch reassignment endpoints. the A* is currently 41% of CPU time per the profile Andrés ran. if we kill 90% of that 41% with the rust port, our per-request CPU drops by ~37%. that translates to roughly 35-38% latency improvement on the median request. p95 should drop from 215 to maybe 135. p99 should drop way more because the slow tail is dominated by A* runs on hard graph queries.

those are projections. they will probably not hold exactly. but the order of magnitude is real. and that's just from the python-extension stage of the project. the full rust rewrite will go farther.

okay. so the technical story is good. the bigger question I'm sitting with at Cardinal is — am I sequencing this right?

time budget. I have, realistically, 60% of my hours for this project. the other 40% is regular team support, on-call rotation, mentoring Andrés, code review, the occasional fire. I am going to be tech lead on this and that means I cannot spend 100% of my time heads-down. I have to do communication overhead. weekly written updates to Daniela, monthly written updates to the wider team, ad-hoc unblocking sessions for Andrés, all of which is real work that doesn't show up in commits.

on top of that: Hugo arrives in 11 days for his first monthly visit. that's a week of being a present son-in-law in the evenings. and I have promised Camila that I'd be home before 7pm during his visits. so my week-of-Hugo work hours are 9-7 with no spillover. that's tighter than my normal 9-9 with occasional 11pm sessions.

and kickboxing — I'm not skipping kickboxing during Hugo's visits, that's already negotiated, that's my Mon/Wed/Fri 7:30-9:30 evenings and Hugo will be at home with Camila those nights. but it does mean the rust work has to fit cleanly into 9-7 with no overflow that week.

so my realistic shipping pace, factoring all that in: A* port (already prototyped) integrated into a deployable pyo3 wheel by end of week 3 (so ten days from now). staging deploy and full perf benchmark by end of week 4. production rollout behind a feature flag by end of week 5. that's about three weeks past where I optimistically projected on monday. that's fine. I'm going to send Daniela a calibrated estimate tomorrow that says 'staging deploy of A* port by end of next week, prod rollout behind feature flag end of week after, full A* port replacing python A* in prod by mid next month.' that's an honest estimate.

then the next phase — the full rewrite to a rust service — kicks off in maybe seven weeks. that's the real beast. that's the 6-month project. I will need Carla back from leave for that to be fully staffed. without her it's just me and Andrés and Andrés is not yet at the point where he can own significant chunks of a rust service unsupervised. so the full rewrite either waits for Carla (preferred) or I lean very heavily into mentoring Andrés to get him to a point where he can ship rust independently within the first two months of the rewrite. the second option doubles my mentoring overhead but probably saves us four weeks of timeline. need to think about that.

the other thing on my mind is — promo. comp committee meets in five weeks. Daniela needs to submit the case in three weeks. she has the draft. she will polish it over the next two weeks. she'll cite the cached-graph fix and the early A* port progress. those are my evidence pieces. the next three weeks of work are the most-watched three weeks I will have at Brújula until I leave or get promoted again. I want to deliver clean. not flashy. clean. I want every line of code I push in the next three weeks to be a line a senior engineer would push.

one thing I want to capture before I close the laptop — the perf number is going to be slightly less impressive in production than in the microbenchmark. I know this. I want to write down why so I don't surprise myself in three weeks. the microbenchmark runs the A* against a pre-loaded graph in memory with a warm process. in production we have graph-loading overhead (we lazy-load CDMX subgraphs by region, so the first request to a cold region pays a 40-80ms penalty), we have FFI overhead on the python-rust boundary (probably 200-500 microseconds per call, negligible per call but real in aggregate), and we have the GIL story — the rust extension can release the GIL during the A* itself which means we get parallelism we didn't have before, which is upside, but the upside is gated on our gunicorn worker config and we have not yet retuned that. realistic production speedup of the A* portion: 10-12x rather than 15x. realistic end-to-end latency improvement: 28-32% on the median, 40-50% at p99. still very good. still worth doing. just smaller numbers than the prototype suggests, and I want to communicate that to Daniela so the comp committee doesn't see 15x in the case write-up and then see 10x in production and feel disappointed.

the other thing — Andrés. I sat with him for ninety minutes this afternoon walking through the rust prototype. he has done some C++ in undergrad and a tiny bit of go, so the borrow checker is a new world to him but he's pattern-matching it onto C++ ownership semantics reasonably well. I had him implement the heuristic function (haversine distance with a graph-density correction) himself while I watched. he finished in maybe forty minutes with three borrow checker errors I helped him resolve. that's promising. if I can get him doing 60-70% of the rust authoring inside three months, with me reviewing, the full-rewrite phase becomes much more tractable even if Carla's parental leave extends. mentoring plan for Andrés: every monday afternoon, ninety minutes, structured pair coding on a real task. every friday afternoon, thirty minutes, code review walk-through where I show him my comments on his week's PRs and explain the reasoning. that's two hours of dedicated mentoring per week, plus the ambient slack/PR review overhead. it's a lot. but it's the highest-leverage two hours I can spend on the team.

okay I'm going to wrap up at Cardinal and head home. Camila is texting me about dinner. I think we're getting tacos al pastor — for her — and a vegetarian taco for me — and we're going to watch a movie. that's the plan. tomorrow morning I'm in early to integrate the wheel into the Brújula CI pipeline. that's at least two hours of yaml sadness. I'll start that with a coffee in hand and a candle on the desk if I'm feeling dramatic.

a longer thought I want to put on paper while it's clear in my head — about the relationship between this project and the senior promo. the comp committee at Brújula does not promote based on a single project. they promote based on a pattern of demonstrated scope expansion over the previous twelve months. so the rust migration is not the case for senior. the case for senior is: five years of growing from junior to mid to senior-mid, two years of leading a small team within the routes group, the cached-graph optimization (visible win), the architecture proposals I've written for the last three quarters, my mentoring track record with Andrés and earlier with Lucía before she went to a different team, my on-call performance during the april 2025 outage, my PR review quality numbers (which Daniela has cited to me twice now as 'top decile of the engineering team'), and my consistent calibration — meaning I shipped what I said I'd ship, when I said I'd ship it, with the perf characteristics I said it would have. the rust migration is the exclamation point on that case. it is not the case itself. that distinction matters because if I treat the rust migration as the only thing keeping the promo alive, I will overinvest in flash and underinvest in the patient discipline the migration actually needs.

related: I want to be careful about the temptation to take on too much in the next three weeks because I think the comp committee is watching. they aren't watching the next three weeks specifically. they're watching the case Daniela writes, which is a synthesis of the past twelve months. so the right move for the next three weeks is the same move it would be if there were no promo: ship the cleanest version of what's needed, take the right amount of care, don't overpromise to make the case look better, don't underpromise to game expectations. just do the work. that's the calibration I want to hold onto.

okay I'm going to wrap up at Cardinal and head home. Camila is texting me about dinner. I think we're getting tacos al pastor — for her — and a vegetarian taco for me — and we're going to watch a movie. that's the plan. tomorrow morning I'm in early to integrate the wheel into the Brújula CI pipeline. that's at least two hours of yaml sadness. I'll start that with a coffee in hand and a candle on the desk if I'm feeling dramatic.

thanks for being a place to write this all out. it actually helps to spell out the schedule constraints in prose because I can see them clearer this way than as a calendar. I'll come back tomorrow if I get stuck on the maturin/manylinux thing.

one last thing before I close. the cat at Cardinal — I just learned its name is Bigotes, the barista told me — has been sitting on the chair across from me for the entire ninety minutes I've been here. it has not moved except to occasionally readjust its tail over its paws. there is something about the patience of cats that I find clarifying when I am trying to think through complicated work. Bigotes does not care about staging deploys or comp committees. Bigotes cares about the warmth of the chair and the slow movement of light across the wood floor of Cardinal. that is a useful corrective. when I get home I am going to be fully present with Camila for dinner. when I get to my desk tomorrow I am going to ship the YAML changes. and the work and the dinner will both happen and the world will keep moving regardless of which one I am thinking about more anxiously. okay. closing the laptop now.

manylinux build step landed in our CI today. took me half a day. the platform team's docker base image is built from a debian-slim that has a slightly weird set of libc versions and I had to align maturin's manylinux2014 target carefully. wheel builds clean now. internal pypi mirror is publishing the routeopt-core wheel. python service can pip install it from the lockfile.

staging deploy with the wheel installed went out at 4pm. the new endpoint that uses the rust A* under the pyo3 wrapper is live behind a feature flag. ran k6 against it for 20 minutes at staging-equivalent load. p95 = 137ms. p99 = 480ms. those are real numbers, not projections. that is dramatically better than where we were two weeks ago.

going to bake it for 48 hours in staging, then start a slow prod rollout next week starting with 5% traffic.

Daniela's reaction: 'that is faster than I had any right to hope for, please go home and have dinner.' I am going home to have dinner.

the one caveat I want to write down so it doesn't get lost in the celebration: the staging load profile is a synthetic k6 mix, not a replay of real prod traffic. real prod has weirder shape — long tails, occasional bursts, mixed regions. I expect production p95 to be in the 150-180ms range, not 137. that's still a 35-40% improvement over the python-only baseline of 215ms. still good. just want to be honest with myself about the gap between staging numbers and prod numbers. I'll mention it in the rollout doc to Daniela so the comp committee, if they see this number cited, knows what its real-world equivalent is.

okay. dinner. Camila is making something with pasta. I can smell garlic. that's enough technical thinking for tonight.

the rollout plan I'm about to write tonight after dinner: monday next week, push the feature flag to 5% of prod traffic and watch the dashboards for 24 hours. tuesday, if everything looks clean, push to 25%. wednesday, 50%. thursday, 100%. friday, decommission the python A* path entirely and remove the feature flag and the dead code. that's a one-week rollout from 0 to 100 with checkpoints at every step. each checkpoint has a clear rollback: flip the flag, no deploy needed, traffic is back on the python path within ten seconds. that's the kind of plan a senior engineer ships. that's the plan I'm going to ship.

Camila went back to work this morning. half day. she walked to the metro at 9, came back at 1pm, said it was 'fine, normal, weird, fine' — that's a direct quote — and immediately took a nap. she slept for two hours. when she woke up she said 'I think I'll keep doing half days for the rest of this week and we'll see.'

I am proud of her. she is being so deliberate about this. she's not pretending to be okay and she's not collapsing. she is calibrating, day by day, what she can carry and adjusting her load. it's so rational. it's so her.

she also told me she's been emailing with her dad about his arrival next week. she's planned the whole thing. he flies in on saturday, she's picking him up alone (I have a kickboxing tournament local thing on saturday I cannot move), I'm meeting them at home for dinner. sunday is family day. monday-friday Hugo will mostly be in the apartment with Nube, watching tv, going for walks, calling friends in São Paulo. (we don't have Nube. we keep saying we should get a dog. we should get a dog.)

she has a list of things she wants Hugo to see in CDMX during this first visit. mostly small things — Cardinal, a couple of museums, a panadería near Parque México. she wants it gentle. no pressure to tour. just rhythms.

anyway. I just wanted to write down that she went back to work today and it went okay. small wins.

the pattern I'm noticing is that almost every meaningful step in her recovery has been initiated by her, not by me. I have been around. I have made meals and lit candles and listened. but the moments that have moved her forward — calling her director about leave, reading her mom's annotated novels, going back to half-days, planning Hugo's visit, framing Bea's photo on the shelf — those have all been her. I am secondary infrastructure. I am the running water and the warm room. she is the person doing the actual recovery work. that's the right shape, I think. a partner who tries to do the recovery for the other partner is doing it wrong. a partner who provides conditions where the other partner can do their own recovery is doing it right. I am doing approximately the right thing.

saturday morning. three weeks since we got home. four weeks since the funeral. Camila is up early — she actually slept through the night for the first time in weeks — and is making pancakes. Hugo lands tomorrow morning. she's doing the last preparations for the apartment today. I have my kickboxing thing this afternoon, a local in-house tournament at Atlas Box, no big deal, just a sparring round-robin with maybe twenty other people. profe asked me to enter and I said yes because I needed something to point my body at this week.

I want to write a closing entry to this stretch before I go to the gym. I keep using this chat as a sort of journal and it's been weirdly useful. I don't know what you remember about previous things I've typed and I'm not going to ask you to recap because that would be artificial. I just want to write where I am right now, top of mind.

so. month one of the post-Bea world. it has been about as bad as I expected and slightly less bad than I feared. the worst day was the day of the funeral itself. the second worst day was the day we arrived in São Paulo and Camila walked into Hugo's apartment and her mom did not come around the corner. the third worst day was the day a week later when Camila found her mom's annotations in the Lispector novel. the worst night was probably the night of the reunião at Hugo's brother's house, where the brigadeiro hit me and I cried in the bathroom for ten minutes.

good things. promotion in motion. I'm tech lead on a major rust migration. the cached-graph python fix shipped and held. the A* rust port is now in staging at p95 = 137ms. that work is the cleanest, sharpest, most technically satisfying work I have done in two years. it's a good year for me at Brújula. I'm going to make senior, I think, and I'm going to lead a flagship project, and I have a manager — Daniela Otero — who is going to write a case for me that gets me there cleanly. that is not nothing. for one month after my mother-in-law's death, that is a lot.

let me list the small wins I keep underrating. I shipped the maturin/manylinux build last tuesday after two days of yaml-induced suffering and the wheel built clean on python 3.11 and 3.12. the CI pipeline caches the rust target dir now so subsequent builds are 90 seconds instead of nine minutes — Andrés did that piece, I just nodded approvingly during the PR review. we have a feature flag wired up that lets us route a percentage of A* calls to the rust path and the rest to the python fallback, which means we can do a slow rollout in production with real-time perf telemetry and an instant kill switch. those are the boring infrastructure pieces that make a migration not catastrophic, and they are all done. the actual A* port code that does the work is maybe 800 lines of rust including tests. small. dense. the kind of code I am proud of.

Daniela sent me the draft of the promo case on thursday for my review. she wants me to flag anything that's off. it's careful and accurate. she cites the cached-graph optimization in detail (architecture decision, perf impact, blast-radius assessment, rollout plan). she cites my tech lead role on the rust migration as evidence of scope expansion. she cites two specific PR reviews where I caught significant bugs before they shipped. she cites my mentoring of Andrés. she does not cite the funeral, which is correct — comp committees should not weigh personal events — but she does mention to me in the email that she has consciously kept my output the past month at a normal-engineer level despite the personal stuff and that the committee should see this as upside not steady state. I read that email three times. I am lucky in my manager.

relationship. okay. tender. honest. we have not had sex in four weeks and I am noticing it but not pressing it. she is grieving. she will come back to herself. five years in we are still made of the right materials. I have no doubts about us. that's not a small thing to be able to say in writing.

family. parents are well in Guadalajara. my brother Mateo and I have spoken twice on the phone in the past two weeks. Hugo is arriving tomorrow. our spare room is fully ready — lamp, sheets, mattress, a bookshelf that I assembled wrong twice and finally got right, blackout curtain, a small framed photo of Bea on the bedside table. I want him to feel that there is a place for him in our home. I think he will.

body. shoulders unknotted, back catching less than last week, sleep is improving — I slept solid five hours straight on wednesday for the first time in a month. mate intake is steady at four cups a day. food is good — Camila and I have been cooking most nights, she's been cooking Brazilian comfort food which I am happy to eat (vegetarian variants, blessedly easy). gym is back to normal pace. I'll find out at the tournament this afternoon if my conditioning has actually returned or if I'm still about 80%. fine either way.

head. quieter than two weeks ago. less hyper-vigilant. I still flinch when my phone rings and the caller is my mom — that's going to take a while. but the daily background hum of dread is reduced. I am thinking about therapy more concretely now. I'll probably make a call this week, or the week after, depending on how Hugo's visit shakes out.

the weirdest thing about this month, retrospectively, is how compressed my emotional bandwidth was while it was happening and how much wider it has become in the last week. for the first two weeks after we got back I had basically two channels — taking care of Camila, and shipping the cached-graph fix. nothing else fit. text messages from friends sat unread for days. a podcast I usually love sat downloaded and unplayed. I forgot to pay one bill and was vaguely surprised by the late notice. it was like the grief and the work used up all the available memory and everything else got swapped to disk. now in the last week I notice the channels opening back up. I texted my friend Pablo and we're getting beers next saturday. I listened to the podcast on my walk home from the gym on friday and laughed out loud at something. I bought a book — Ted Chiang's new collection — that I had been meaning to buy for months. small re-engagements with the world. those are the pulse coming back.

the other thing I want to write down is a line I've been carrying around since the funeral. on the day of the burial, when we got back to Hugo's apartment afterward and everyone was sitting around eating a buffet someone had put together, Camila's tía Lourdes — who is sixty-something, sharp, no-nonsense — pulled me aside in the hallway and said in Portuguese, 'cuide bem dela. e cuide bem de você. as duas coisas, juntas.' take good care of her. and take good care of yourself. both things, together. I have been thinking about that line at least once a day for four weeks. it sounds simple in writing. it is not simple. the temptation when your partner is grieving is to disappear yourself entirely into her care. tía Lourdes' line says no — both things, together. I have been trying to honor that. I think I have done okay. I have not disappeared myself. I have gone to kickboxing. I have shipped code I am proud of. I have eaten well. I have called my own mother. and I have, when she has needed me, been entirely available to Camila. both things, together. that's the orientation.

okay. you've been a strange and consistent presence in this stretch. I don't know if you'll remember any of this — I assume you won't, that's just how these things work — but I'm Seb and I just buried my mother-in-law and got promoted and started a new technical chapter all in the same month and the order of those was not my choice and the universe did not consult me. and yet. here I am. coffee in hand. mate cup at my elbow. cedar candle from last sunday burned down to a stump that Camila will replace tomorrow. and I am, with all the appropriate caveats, okay.

a longer reflection because the candle is half-burned and I am not going to let myself stand up until it is done. things I have learned in the past four weeks that I want to keep. one: presence is more important than performance. for two weeks I kept catching myself wanting to do something visibly grief-supportive — buy a thing, plan a trip, organize a memorial, dramatically reorganize my schedule. Camila did not need any of that. she needed me to sit on the couch with her and not leave when she cried. the sit-and-don't-leave was the entire job. I had to keep relearning it. two: people I would not have predicted to show up showed up. Doña Marta from upstairs, the panadería owner Marina, two engineers I have worked with for years and never had a non-work conversation with — they all showed up in small specific ways and I will not forget who. there is also a quiet list I am keeping, mostly empty but not entirely, of people I had counted as close who did not show up. I am not punishing them. I am just noting it. three: language matters. my fluency in Portuguese went from 'workable' to 'load-bearing' in two weeks. I had to write condolence cards. I had to talk to Hugo at 2am when he could not sleep. I had to make small talk with cousins I had met once and could not remember the names of. and the language carried me. languages are tools you don't know are essential until they are. four: my body is a real input to my mental health. the days I went to kickboxing were the days I slept better. the days I ran were the days I was kinder to Camila. the days I ate vegetables instead of cheese bread for breakfast were the days I thought clearly. these are not insights, they are reminders. I keep needing to remind myself.

five: my work is not a refuge. it is also not a distraction. it is a place where I am competent and where I make a small concrete contribution to a small concrete thing and that competence and contribution are part of who I am. when I shipped the cached-graph fix I felt myself become more recognizable to myself. that was useful. but I had to be careful. I had a couple of evenings where I worked on the rust prototype until midnight when what Camila needed was for me to come to bed at 10. I caught myself. I corrected. work is a place where I am competent. it is not the place where I am most needed. those are two different things. I want to remember the distinction.

six and last: the world keeps going. CDMX traffic was bad on tuesday because of a march. someone at Brújula got a new dog and brought it into the office and it tried to eat a power cable. a friend of Camila's announced a pregnancy. the universe does not pause for grief. it keeps producing news, both good and trivial, that you have to absorb on top of the grief. you absorb it. or you don't. either way the news keeps coming. and somehow that's a comfort, weirdly. the grief is not the only thing happening. it is one thing among many things happening, and the many other things are also yours to attend to.

okay. that's the closing entry. I am going to go.

I'm going to close this and go eat pancakes with my partner who is the love of my life. she just called me to the kitchen. she's calling me 'amor.' that's the first time she has called me 'amor' in four weeks. I noticed it through the closed bedroom door. I am going to go.

back later if I have a question. probably tomorrow about whether to pick Hugo up at the airport with flowers or with nothing. I haven't decided. flowers feel performative. but no flowers feels like I missed a beat. I'll think about it on the bike ride to Atlas Box. talk soon.
